setup_simulation_howto.user: How do I set up a simulation on the platform?
setup_simulation_howto.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation for simulation setup. I'll fetch the product documentation (circuit-simulation and single-cell-simulation) so I can give step-by-step instructions.## How to set up a simulation on the Open Brain Platform

Below is a concise, practical guide you can follow whether you use the web UI (recommended for interactive setup) or the OBI-one programmatic interface. I first looked up the platform documentation for circuit and single-cell simulations to ensure the steps match the platform schema and workflow.

---

## Quick overview (two supported flows)
- Web interface — easiest for interactive building of small microcircuit simulations (schema-driven GUI with a preview and validation).
- OBI-one (programmatic) — for reproducible/scripted generation of SONATA simulation configs (single-cell and circuit workflows).

---

## Step-by-step — Web interface (Small Microcircuit Simulation)

### 1) Start a new simulation
- Go to Workflows → Simulate → choose \Small Microcircuit\ as the simulation type.
- Select a circuit model from your project or public data.

### 2) Build the configuration (Configuration tab)
- Use the left column to open configuration categories (grouped by purpose).
- For each section:
  - Click Add (or select an existing element) to add configuration elements.
  - Edit parameters in the middle panel.
  - Use the right-hand model preview to confirm you’re targeting the correct populations and anatomy.

Important fields to set (schema-driven; defaults exist):
- initialize.circuit — the SONATA circuit (or pick one from project/public).
- initialize.node_set — which neurons to simulate (must be biophysical for circuit sims).
- initialize.simulation_length — simulation duration (ms).
- initialize.v_init — initial membrane potential (mV).
- initialize.extracellular_calcium_concentration — [mM].
- timestep / recording dt — simulation timestep and recording interval.
- stimuli — add current clamp, Poisson inputs, or spike stimuli as needed.
- recordings — SomaVoltageRecording or TimeWindowSomaVoltageRecording.

### 3) Validate configuration
- The UI validates against the JSON schema and highlights errors.
- Resolve any validation errors (required fields, invalid ranges, node_set type mismatches).

Tips:
- The schema is generated from the circuit model — available options depend on the selected model.
- Start simple (few stimuli, short duration) for initial testing.

### 4) Generate a simulation campaign
- Once valid, click Generate Campaign (or similar).
- The platform will create a SimulationCampaign and a set of Simulations (one per parameter combination) and return a campaign ID. Save this ID.

### 5) Run simulations (Simulations tab)
- Go to Simulations tab to see generated simulations and their status.
- Select simulation(s) and click Run.
- Monitor statuses (Created → Running → Completed / Error).

### 6) Inspect and download results
- Available outputs typically include:
  - Voltage traces (time series)
  - Spike times
  - SONATA / HDF5 reports, optionally NWB files
- Preview files in the UI or download for offline analysis.

---

## Step-by-step — OBI-one programmatic interface (recommended for scripting and reproducibility)

### 1) Build an OBI-one simulation config
- Create a CircuitSimulationScanConfig (for circuits) or MEModelSimulationSingleConfig (for single-cell).
- Key initialize block fields:
  - circuit: Circuit (path) or CircuitFromID
  - node_set: reference to neuron set (must be biophysical)
  - simulation_length, v_init, extracellular_calcium_concentration, random_seed
  - timestep, spike_location (\soma\ or \AIS")
- Add neuron_sets dictionary (AllNeurons, IDNeuronSet, PredefinedNeuronSet, property-based sets, etc.).
- Add stimuli blocks and recordings blocks according to the schema.

### 2) Use GenerateSimulationTask (obi_one)
- The GenerateSimulationTask converts OBI-one config → SONATA simulation_config.json + node_sets.json and stages circuit files.
- It also inserts synaptic mechanisms automatically:
  - ProbAMPANMDA_EMS (excitatory)
  - ProbGABAAB_EMS (inhibitory)
- Synaptic manipulations (SynapticMgManipulation, ScaleAcetylcholineUSESynapticManipulation) can be added as connection_overrides.

### 3) Execute simulations
- Use the platform execution backend (BlueCelluLab / NEURON with MPI support) to run generated SONATA configs.
- For small circuits (≤20 biophysical neurons) execution is optimized for rapid testing.
- Larger circuits require more resources and distributed execution.

### 4) Read outputs programmatically
- Outputs are produced as SONATA/HDF5 traces and spike files and can also be converted to NWB.
- Use the OBI-one helper libraries/examples to load and analyze output files.

---

## Minimum viable configuration checklist (to run a first test)
- [x] initialize.circuit (Circuit or CircuitFromID)
- [x] initialize.node_set (biophysical neuron set)
- [x] initialize.simulation_length (e.g. 1000 ms)
- [x] initialize.v_init (e.g. -80 mV)
- [x] timestep (e.g. 0.025 ms)
- [x] At least one recording (SomaVoltageRecording or spikes)
- [x] Validate schema with UI or via OBI-one generation task

---

## Best practices and tips
- Start with short simulations and a small neuron set to validate setup and performance.
- Use the model preview in the UI to confirm populations and node sets before generating campaigns.
- Check validation errors carefully — the schema enforces types, ranges, and required fields.
- Save the campaign ID after generation — it links the configuration to generated simulations.
- Test parameter ranges incrementally before large batch runs.
- Use the examples directory (platform docs / examples) to copy working templates for common tasks.

---

## Where to look in the documentation (pages I consulted)
- \Small Microcircuit Simulation (Web Interface)\ — step-by-step UI workflow and configuration categories.
- \OBI-one: Small Circuit Simulations\ — OBI-one config structure, required fields, synaptic mechanisms, and GenerateSimulationTask internals.
- \Tips\ — practical advice (start simple, validate, save campaign IDs).
- \OBI-one: Programmatic Single Cell Simulations\ — single-cell programmatic examples and config structure.

---

If you want, I can:
- Walk through configuring a specific circuit you have (I can help fill required fields and create a minimal valid config).
- Generate an OBI-one JSON config template for a short example simulation (provide circuit ID or path and which neuron set to simulate).
- Show an example minimal SONATA simulation_config.json based on typical defaults.

submit_jobs_digital_brain.user: How can I submit jobs and get responses from the Digital Brain? What language or syntax can I use to communicate with it?
submit_jobs_digital_brain.expected_output: you submit simulation jobs through the Workflows > Simulate interface (graphical UI) or programmatically via Jupyter notebooks. The platform also has an AI chat assistant that accepts natural language queries for data exploration and literature search.

I'll search for related docs like \simulation", \launch-notebook", \simulation API", and \simulationExecution".## Short answer — how to send jobs and get responses
You submit simulations (jobs) to the Digital Brain using the platform’s simulation workflows. There are two main ways:

- Web interface (Workflows → Simulate) — use the UI to build a simulation configuration, generate a simulation campaign, run simulations, and view/download results. The web UI validates your configuration and returns a campaign ID and simulation statuses/results.
- Programmatic interface (OBI‑one) — create a configuration in JSON following the platform/OBI‑one JSON schema (SONATA-style simulation config), generate a SimulationCampaign, and run simulations via the execution backend. OBI‑one exposes a FastAPI-style programmatic interface and library classes you can use to generate and run configurations.

Responses (job status, outputs, and files) are returned via the platform simulation UI and stored as simulation results (voltage traces, spike files, NWB, HDF5 reports). Programmatic runs produce the same simulation outputs and campaign/simulation IDs.

---

## What language / syntax to use
- Primary format: JSON configuration files following the platform’s JSON schema (SONATA-compatible). The configuration is schema-driven and validated by the platform.
  - Typical top-level fields and blocks in a circuit configuration include:
    - initialize: circuit (Circuit or CircuitFromID), node_set (must reference a biophysical neuron set), simulation_length (ms), v_init, timestep, random_seed, extracellular_calcium_concentration, spike_location
    - node_sets.json: explicit node set definitions (AllNeurons, IDNeuronSet, PropertyNeuronSet, PredefinedNeuronSet, CombinedNeuronSet)
    - stimuli: current clamp definitions, spike trains (Poisson, synchronous, etc.)
    - recordings: report definitions (soma voltage traces, spike outputs)
    - connection_overrides / manipulations: synaptic manipulations and overrides
  - The platform writes SONATA-style outputs (simulation_config.json, node_sets.json) and uses SONATA as the execution config format.

- OBI‑one Python classes / objects (programmatic helper API):
  - Use OBI‑one classes such as CircuitSimulationSingleConfig and GenerateSimulationTask to build JSON configs programmatically (these classes serialize to the JSON/SONATA config expected by execution).
  - OBI‑one workflows generate:
    - simulation_config.json (SONATA format)
    - node_sets.json
  - Execution uses the BlueCelluLab/NEURON backend (MPI support) under the hood; you do not hand the raw MPI calls — you generate configs and request execution.

- API docs / OpenAPI:
  - The OBI‑one codebase provides a FastAPI service (with auto-generated docs) you can run locally for programmatic workflows. The generated OpenAPI docs show exact request/response schemas when available.

---

## Typical workflow (summary)
1. Build/compose a JSON configuration (via web UI forms or programmatic OBI‑one classes).
2. Validate configuration (platform/OBI‑one validates against the JSON schema).
3. Generate a SimulationCampaign (the platform returns a campaign ID).
4. The campaign is expanded into individual Simulations (one per parameter combination).
5. Launch execution for selected simulations. Monitor status: Created → Running → Completed/Error.
6. Retrieve outputs: voltage traces, spike files, NWB, HDF5 reports, and other reports available in the Simulations tab or programmatically via the API.

---

## Common file/formats you will work with
- JSON (configuration files; SONATA-compatible simulation_config.json)
- node_sets.json (node set definitions)
- SONATA format outputs (spikes, reports)
- NWB and HDF5 for standardized results and spike files
- Standard trace/time-series files for voltages and spike times

---

## Where to find exact schemas and examples
- Use the OBI‑one repository/docs and the platform “Circuit Simulation” / “Small Microcircuit Simulation” documentation to see concrete JSON schema examples and example notebooks. The OBI‑one library contains classes and example notebooks (examples/E_run_small_microcircuit, examples/C_forms/circuit_simulation) showing how to programmatically create configs and run them.
- If you run an OBI‑one FastAPI instance locally, use its auto-generated OpenAPI docs to inspect exact request and response schemas.

---

simulate_simple_neuron.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation for single-cell simulation to give accurate, current steps.## Short answer
Yes — the platform supports single-neuron (ME-model) simulations. You can simulate a single simple neuron for 1 second (1000 ms) using either the web UI or the programmatic OBI-one interface.

Below are concise, actionable steps and the minimum settings you need.

---

## Where to read the official docs
- **Single-cell simulation overview & configuration** — see the platform docs for single-cell simulation: [Single-cell simulation — Overview & Configuration](#)  
  (Documentation entries returned from the platform: **single-cell-simulation/index** and **single-cell-simulation/configuration** — see the platform docs for full detail.)
- **Programmatic (OBI-one) single-cell workflow** — see: **single-cell-simulation/obi-one**.

(If you want I can open the exact documentation pages for you — tell me which one and I’ll fetch it.)

---

## Quick steps — Web UI (recommended for quick tests)

1. Open the Single-Cell Simulation product in the platform.
2. Create a new simulation and fill the **Info** fields:
   - **Name**: a unique name.
   - **Description**: optional.
3. Configure Experimental Setup:
   - **Maximum time / Simulation length**: **1000 ms** (1 second).
   - **Time step** (dt): choose e.g. **0.025 ms** (default options include 0.05 ms; smaller dt → more accurate but slower).
   - **Temperature**: leave default or set as needed (e.g., 34 °C).
   - **Initial voltage (vinit)**: default **-80 mV** (change if needed).
4. Choose the model to simulate:
   - Select a **MEModel** (Morphology + Electrophysiology) from the catalogue or upload/select an MEModel entity.
5. Define stimulation protocol:
   - **Stimulus type**: e.g., **Constant current clamp**.
   - **Injection location**: e.g., *soma[0]*.
   - **Amplitude / Duration**: set amplitude (nA) and duration within the 1000 ms window.
6. Recording:
   - Add one or more recording locations (e.g., soma) to record **voltage** traces.
7. Run the simulation:
   - Submit / Launch. The backend (NEURON / BlueCelluLab) will run the single neuron simulation.
8. Inspect outputs:
   - Voltage traces, spike times, NWB export and report files are available after the run.

Minimum required fields for a 1-second run: model selection, simulation length = 1000 ms, at least one recording location. Stimulus can be omitted if you only want spontaneous activity.

---

## Quick steps — Programmatic (OBI-one) interface

1. Build a MEModel single config using MEModelSimulationSingleConfig (or MEModelSimulationScanConfig for parameter sweeps).
2. Key fields (example minimal config):
   - initialize.circuit: reference the MEModel (MEModelFromID or MEModel object)
   - initialize.simulation_length: **1000.0** (ms)
   - initialize.v_init: -80.0 (mV) — optional
   - timestep: 0.025 (ms) — optional (default 0.025)
   - stimuli: define a current clamp stimulus if required (duration within 1000 ms)
   - recordings: SomaVoltageRecording (or other)
3. Use GenerateSimulationTask to convert the config to SONATA and then run using the simulation_execution module (backend: NEURON / BlueCelluLab).
4. Retrieve results (voltage traces, spikes, NWB) once execution finishes.

Refer to the OBI-one single-cell examples (examples/F_single_cell_simulations/) in the documentation for code examples and templates.

---

## Important defaults & tips
- Time units: milliseconds — 1000 ms = 1 s.
- Default timestep: platform docs mention typical defaults like 0.025 ms (single-cell code examples use 0.025).
- Default initial voltage: -80 mV (change if you want e.g., -65 mV).
- Outputs: voltage traces, spike times, NWB files, reports.
- If you need synaptic inputs or synaptome-style single-cell runs, there are extra config options for synaptic locations and frequencies.
- Use larger dt only for coarse exploratory runs; reduce dt for accurate spike waveforms.

---

## Example minimal parameter list (for your reference)
- simulation_length: **1000.0** (ms)
- timestep: **0.025** (ms)
- v_init: **-80.0** (mV)
- stimulus: (optional) constant current clamp — amplitude e.g. 0.1 nA, duration ≤ 1000 ms
- recordings: SomaVoltageRecording (neuron_set → your single MEModel)

---

- Draft a minimal OBI-one JSON config for a 1-second current-clamp single-cell run that you can paste into the programmatic interface.

simulate_me_model_howto.ai_response:
I'll search the platform documentation for guidance on running ME-model simulations and accessing notebooks. I'll look in the product documentation for single-cell or ME-model simulation guides.## Summary — how to go from a selected ME‑model to running simulations

Below I summarize the supported ways to run ME‑model (MEModel) simulations on the platform and where to find example notebooks and programmatic interfaces. All items below are taken directly from the platform documentation.

---

## Main options

- **Web interface**  
  - Use the platform's single‑cell simulation UI to create and run an MEModel simulation with a guided configuration (info, experimental setup, stimulation protocol, recordings).  
  - Key configuration fields: **Maximum time**, **Time step**, **Temperature**, **v_init**, **Seed**, **Injection location**, **Stimulus type/protocol**, **Recording locations**.

- **Programmatic (OBI-one) interface**  
  - Use **OBI-one** to generate and run simulations programmatically. OBI-one converts the MEModel configuration into SONATA simulation configs and executes via the platform back end.
  - Key classes/tasks in OBI-one:
    - **MEModelSimulationSingleConfig** — the configuration object for single‑cell MEModel runs.
    - **GenerateSimulationTask** — converts the config into SONATA files and stages execution.
    - **simulation_execution** module — handles execution (BlueCelluLab / NEURON back end).

---

## Step‑by‑step (single cell MEModel) — web UI or OBI‑one

1. From your selected ME‑model (MEModel), choose “Run single cell simulation” (web UI) or create a **MEModelSimulationSingleConfig** (OBI-one).
2. Configure the simulation:
   - Basic info: **Name**, **Description**.
   - Experimental setup: **simulation_length** (ms), **timestep** (ms), **v_init** (mV), **temperature**, random **seed**.

   - Recordings: add recording locations (section + offset) and choose whether to record currents or only voltage.

synaptic_input_meaning.user: What does the "synaptic input" on the current page mean? Is it a signal input from a specified position?
synaptic_input_meaning.ai_response:
I'll search the platform documentation for the term \synaptic input\ so I can give the exact platform definition. I'll query the product documentation.No direct hit for \synaptic_input". I'll broaden the search to \synaptic\ and \input\ separately.## Short answer
On this platform, **\synaptic input\** means a configured synapse (or set of synapses) placed on the neuron's morphology that is driven/activated by a spike train or event generator. It is *not* the same as a point current injection — it is a synaptic activation at specified synaptic locations on the morphology (section + offset), with configurable timing/frequency and synaptic properties.

## Details (from platform documentation)
- **Context:** The Synaptic Inputs step appears for *single neuron synaptome* simulations (single-neuron models that include explicit synapses).
- **What you specify:**
  - **Synaptic locations:** where on the morphology the synapse(s) are located (i.e., a section such as `dend[5]`, plus an offset along that section).
  - **Frequency / timing:** the activation pattern (e.g., Poisson rate, specified spike times, etc.) used to drive each synaptic input.
  - **Synaptic properties:** parameters of each synapse (release, conductances, receptor types, etc.).
- **Effect during simulation:** the configured synapses produce postsynaptic currents/voltages at their anatomical locations when driven — i.e., realistic synaptic inputs distributed over the morphology rather than a single injected current at the soma.

## How this differs from a spatial \signal at a position"
- If by “signal input from a specified position” you mean a direct current injection into a single point on the membrane (like a current clamp at soma[0] or dend[0]), that is a different stimulus type (current clamp).
- **Synaptic inputs** are anatomically placed synapses that produce synaptic conductance changes and postsynaptic currents at the specified morphological positions; they simulate synaptic transmission rather than direct injected current.

## Practical guidance
- To place or edit a synaptic input on the page:
  - Choose the section and offset on the 3D morphology viewer (or enter them manually).
  - Set the activation pattern (frequency, seed, or explicit timestamps).
  - Configure synaptic mechanism / parameters (if available).
- If you want a single localized electrical stimulus instead of a synapse, use a **current clamp** (injection location + amplitude/duration) instead.

---

## How the platform runs single‑cell simulation files (high level)
- Single‑cell simulations use the **MEModel** (morphology + electrophysiology) configuration and are converted into a SONATA simulation config by the platform’s OBI‑one workflows.
- Execution uses the platform backend (BlueCelluLab with NEURON; MPI support for parallel runs).
- The workflow has two main stages:
  1. **Generate** a simulation configuration (MEModel → SONATA simulation_config.json, node_sets.json, etc.) using the GenerateSimulationTask (OBI‑one).
  2. **Execute** the generated simulation using the platform execution backend (BlueCelluLab / NEURON).

---

## Option A — Programmatic (OBI‑one / Python) — recommended for running a .py simulation script
Typical flow (programmatic) to run a file like passive_neuron_1dend.py:

1. Prepare the MEModel / input
   - Ensure your script references a valid MEModel object or an entity ID (MEModelFromID) representing the neuron you want to simulate.
   - The configuration must include an initialize block that points to the MEModel (circuit) to simulate.

2. Configure simulation parameters (these fields are required/commonly used)
   - **circuit**: MEModel (path or entity id)
   - **simulation_length** (ms) — total duration (default examples: 1000 ms)
   - **v_init** (mV) — initial membrane voltage (default: -80.0 mV)
   - **timestep / dt** (ms) — simulation time step (typical: 0.025 ms)
   - **random_seed** — for reproducibility
   - **spike_location** — \soma\ or \AIS\ if spike detection is needed

3. Define stimulation / recording
   - Stimulus types supported: current clamp (constant, step, sinusoidal, linear), Ornstein–Uhlenbeck, Poisson spike inputs, etc.
   - Recording options: SomaVoltageRecording, TimeWindowSomaVoltageRecording (dt for recordings)
   - Select injection location (e.g., soma[0], dend[0]) and recording locations (section + offset)

4. Use OBI‑one GenerateSimulationTask
   - In your script call the OBI‑one routine that converts your MEModel config into SONATA simulation files. This produces:
     - simulation_config.json (SONATA)
     - node_sets.json (if applicable)
     - any staged circuit files

5. Execute the simulation
   - Call the platform execution layer provided by the OBI‑one library (the simulation_execution module) to run the generated SONATA config on the BlueCelluLab backend (NEURON).
   - The execution module handles distribution (MPI) and result gathering.

6. Collect outputs
   - The run produces voltage traces, spike times, and standardized NWB/HDF5 outputs depending on configuration.
   - Review generated reports and trace files after completion.

Notes:
- The platform includes programmatic examples and an examples directory for single‑cell simulations (see documentation examples/F_single_cell_simulations/).
- OBI‑one classes mentioned in docs: MEModelSimulationSingleConfig and the GenerateSimulationTask → these are the programmatic entry points to create and run the simulation configuration.

---

## Option B — Web interface (UI) — easier if you prefer a form-based workflow
If you prefer the web UI flow (no code):

1. Create a new Single‑Cell Simulation
   - Open the Single‑Cell Simulation product and click “Create Simulation” (the docs list a Getting Started / web UI guide).

2. Fill the Info form
   - **Name** (unique), **Description** (optional)

3. Experimental setup
   - Maximum time (simulation length in ms)
   - Time step (dt)
   - Temperature, vinit (initial voltage), random seed

4. Stimulation protocol
   - Choose injection location (select via 3D morphology view or by name: soma[0], dend[0], etc.)
   - Choose stimulus type and set amplitudes/duration/offsets (the UI provides templates: Step, AP_WAVEFORM, Subthreshold step, Fire pattern, etc.)

5. Recording
   - Add recording locations via 3D visualization or form; set dt for recording traces.

6. Run
   - Submit the simulation; the platform will generate the SONATA config and run it on the internal execution backend (BlueCelluLab).
   - After completion, download voltage traces / spike data / NWB outputs from the results page.

---

## Minimal example: what your script / config must specify
At minimum, whether programmatic or UI, provide:

## Checklist before running passive_neuron_1dend.py
- [ ] Confirm the file **passive_neuron_1dend.py** points to a valid MEModel or contains the MEModelFromID entry.

## Common troubleshooting tips
- Simulation fails or stalls:
  - Check dt/time step — too small increases compute; too large may destabilize integration.
  - Check v_init and ionic mechanism compatibility with the MEModel.

brain_malformation_simulation.ai_response:
I'll check the platform documentation for simulation capabilities and guidance on 3D brain models and anatomical modifications.## Short answer — feasibility summary
- The platform supports biophysically detailed neuron and circuit simulations (single neurons, paired neurons, small circuits, microcircuits) and provides workflows for preparing morphologies (**neuron skeletonization**) and building simulation configurations (**OBI-one** circuit workflows).  
- The platform documentation does **not** describe built‑in tools for anatomical deformation, biomechanical simulation, cerebrospinal‑fluid (CSF) flow, or surgical planning workflows for structural malformations such as Chiari. That means you cannot currently run an integrated MRI‑to‑surgical‑planning deformation simulation purely inside the platform.  
- However, you can represent the electrophysiological consequences of anatomic changes by: (A) preprocessing the anatomy externally (mesh deformation, tissue displacement, voxel/mesh → neuron placement), (B) importing the resulting morphologies / SONATA circuits or ME‑models into the platform, and (C) running electrophysiology / circuit simulations with OBI‑one.

See these docs I checked for platform capabilities: **Explore Data Guide**, **Neuron Skeletonization (OBI‑one)**, and **OBI‑one: Small Circuit Simulations / Small Microcircuit Simulation (Web Interface)**.

---

## What the platform can do (relevant items from docs)
- Data types available: **morphologies**, **electrophysiology**, **densities**, **E‑models**, **ME‑models**, **circuits**, **synaptomes** (Explore Data Guide).  
- Morphology preparation: **neuron skeletonization** workflows available to process neuron morphologies and prepare neurons for simulation.  
- Simulation engine: **OBI‑one** supports generating SONATA simulation configs and running circuit simulations (single, pair, small microcircuit, microcircuit, region, whole_brain scales are defined). Small microcircuits (≤20 biophysical neurons) are fully supported with a web interface and programmatic tools; larger circuits are possible but need more resources.  
- Simulation configuration: OBI‑one generates SONATA simulation_config.json and node_sets.json, configures synaptic mechanisms, stimuli, recordings, and supports synaptic manipulations.

---

## Main limitation for Chiari malformation surgical planning
- The documentation does **not** show any native support for:
  - 3D anatomical deformation or surgical manipulation (e.g., cerebellar tonsil herniation modeling),
  - fluid dynamics / CSF flow simulation,
  - tissue mechanics / finite‑element biomechanical simulation,
  - integrated MRI‑based surgical planning pipelines.
- Those parts must be done with specialized medical imaging / biomechanics tools external to the platform. The platform can then be used for electrophysiological/neuronal simulations after you import the anatomically modified data.

---

## Recommended high‑level workflow to represent a Chiari malformation on the platform

1. Clarify objective (I can help refine this):
   - Are you modelling structural displacement only (anatomical geometry), or do you need CSF flow / intracranial pressure, or electrophysiological effects (e.g., local circuit changes, seizure risk)?

   - From the deformed anatomy, derive a neuronal microstructure representation:

4. Configure simulations with OBI‑one:
   - Define a node_set (the neurons to simulate) and simulation parameters (simulation_length, v_init, timestep).

multi_neuron_simulation.user: How can I run a multi-neuron simulation on the platform?
multi_neuron_simulation.ai_response:
I'll search the platform documentation for the circuit/multi-neuron simulation guides. I'll fetch relevant documentation pages now.## How to run a multi-neuron (circuit) simulation on the Open Brain Platform

Below is a concise, step-by-step guide based only on the platform documentation I retrieved. You can run multi-neuron simulations either via the web interface (recommended for interactive use) or programmatically with **OBI-one**.

---

## 1) Choose your path: Web interface or OBI-one (programmatic)
- Web Interface — use the **Small Microcircuit Simulation (Web Interface)** workflow to configure, validate, generate campaigns and run simulations from the browser.
- OBI-one — use the **OBI-one: Small Circuit Simulations** programmatic API to generate SONATA simulation configs and run them (suitable for automation, notebooks, and reproducible pipelines).

---

## 2) Typical workflow (both methods)
1. Configure the circuit and simulation parameters
2. Generate a Simulation Campaign (creates one SimulationCampaign + many Simulations for swept parameters)
3. Run simulations (select simulations from the campaign and execute)
4. Monitor progress and retrieve outputs (voltage traces, spike times, reports)
5. Analyze / download results

---

## 3) Key configuration elements you must set
(Names below reflect the schema fields used by the platform documentation.)

- **Circuit**
  - A SONATA circuit entity or a path to SONATA circuit files.
  - In OBI-one this can be specified as a **Circuit** or **CircuitFromID**.

- **Neuron sets**
  - Defines which biophysical neurons participate. Must reference a *biophysical* neuron set.
  - Examples: **AllNeurons**, **IDNeuronSet**, property-based sets, or **PredefinedNeuronSet**.
  - The initialize.node_set must point to a biophysical neuron set.

- **Simulation duration & time step**
  - **simulation_length** (ms) — default: 1000.0 ms
  - **timestep** (ms, dt) — default: 0.025 ms

- **Initial conditions & reproducibility**
  - **v_init** (mV) — default: -80.0 mV
  - **random_seed** — default: 1

- **Extracellular variables**
  - **extracellular_calcium_concentration** (mM) — default: 1.1 mM

- **Recordings & spike detection**
  - **SomaVoltageRecording** or **TimeWindowSomaVoltageRecording** to capture voltages
  - Spike location: **soma** or **AIS**

- **Stimuli**
  - Current-clamp (constant, linear, sinusoidal, OU, etc.) or spike-based (Poisson, synchronous, sinusoidal Poisson)
  - Can target neuron sets to stimulate subpopulations

- **Synaptic mechanisms & manipulations**
  - Circuits automatically include platform synaptic mechanisms (e.g., **ProbAMPANMDA_EMS**, **ProbGABAAB_EMS**)
  - Optional synaptic manipulations: **SynapticMgManipulation**, **ScaleAcetylcholineUSESynapticManipulation**, connect/disconnect ops

---

## 4) Web Interface: step-by-step (high level)
- Open your project → Workflows → Simulate → choose **Small Microcircuit**.
- Select a circuit model (from your project or public data).
- Use the Configuration tab:
  - Left column: categories; middle: detailed fields; right: model preview.
  - Add/edit neuron sets, stimuli, recordings, synaptic manipulations.
  - Resolve validation errors shown by the UI.
- Click **Generate Campaign**:
  - The UI validates the config and creates a campaign ID.
- Switch to Simulations tab:
  - View generated simulations (one per parameter combination).
  - Select simulations and click **Run**.
- Monitor running simulations and download outputs when complete.

Reference doc: **Small Microcircuit Simulation (Web Interface)** (documentationProduct).

---

## 5) OBI-one (programmatic) workflow (high level)
- Use the OBI-one classes/tasks:
  - **CircuitSimulationSingleConfig**: build the JSON config programmatically.
  - **GenerateSimulationTask**: resolves the circuit, writes SONATA simulation_config.json and node_sets.json, configures synapses, stimuli, and reports.
- Ensure the config includes:
  - initialize.circuit (Circuit or CircuitFromID)
  - initialize.node_set (biophysical)
  - simulation_length, v_init, random_seed, timestep, extracellular_calcium_concentration
  - neuron_sets, stimuli, recordings, synaptic_manipulations (optional)
- After GenerateSimulationTask produces SONATA config files, use the platform’s execution backend:
  - Execution backend supports MPI distribution (BlueCelluLab/NEURONMPI) and gathers outputs across ranks.
- Outputs: voltage traces, spike times (HDF5), NWB files and reports.

Reference doc: **OBI-one: Small Circuit Simulations** (documentationProduct).

---

## 6) Expected outputs
- Voltage traces (time-series) for recorded neurons
- Spike files (HDF5) — spike times for populations
- NWB files (standardized data)
- Simulation reports and analysis artifacts
- Connectivity data and generated SONATA config files (simulation_config.json, node_sets.json)

---

## 7) Practical tips (from platform docs)
- Start simple: test with a small neuron subset and short simulation length.
- Validate configuration before generating a campaign; the UI shows validation errors.
- Use the model preview in the UI to confirm node/edge populations.
- Save the campaign ID — it links configuration → generated simulations.
- Batch runs: you can select and run multiple simulations from the same campaign.
- Incremental testing: sweep small parameter ranges first.

Reference doc: **Tips** (documentationProduct).

---

## 8) When to prefer each route
- Use the web interface if you prefer a guided, schema-driven visual configuration and one-off or interactive runs.
- Use OBI-one (programmatic) for automation, reproducibility, notebook-driven experiments, and to generate SONATA configs for custom execution pipelines.

---

If you want, I can:
- Walk through building a minimal web-UI configuration (fields to set) for a simple 3–5 neuron test circuit; or
- Create a runnable OBI-one JSON configuration (CircuitSimulationSingleConfig) for a small example circuit (you can then generate the SONATA config with GenerateSimulationTask).

Which would you prefer — a web-UI walkthrough or a programmatic OBI-one example?

## Goals and high-level comparison
You want to simulate two conditions in the mouse hippocampus and compare them:

- Simulation scale: region-scale vs microcircuit vs paired neurons

## Proposed simulation options (summary + tradeoffs)

### Option A — Region-scale, atlas-based hippocampal simulation (high realism)
- What it does: build an atlas-based model of the hippocampal region (CA fields + DG volume), with anatomically-informed populations and inter-population connectivity.
- Pros: closest to your stated goal (region-level comparisons); can analyze network dynamics and spatial patterns.
- Cons: computationally heavy; needs more detailed data and run-time; may require manual building/validation steps.

### Option B — Small microcircuit (recommended first step)
- What it does: simulate a canonical hippocampal microcircuit (e.g., CA1 microcircuit with pyramidal cells + interneurons + extrinsic inputs) for both conditions.
- Pros: much faster to iterate; easier to test mechanistic hypotheses (synapse loss, excitability changes); platform shared state currently supports generating small microcircuit simulation configs.
- Cons: less spatial/atlas detail than a full region model.
- Good for: validating hypotheses, sensitivity analyses, preliminary comparisons between aging and TBI.

### Option C — Paired-neuron experiments (mechanistic, minimal)
- What it does: paired-cell simulations (e.g., pyramidal→interneuron) to test synaptic physiology changes (LTP/LTD, failure rates).
- Pros: fastest and simplest; useful for testing synapse-level hypotheses.
- Cons: far from network-level behavior.

---

## Suggested mechanistic manipulations to represent each condition
(These are suggestions — I will implement only what you confirm.)

- Aging hippocampus (typical model features)
  - Reduced synaptic density (percentage reduction in excitatory synapses)
  - Altered intrinsic excitability (e.g., small changes in input resistance / spike threshold)
  - Slower synaptic kinetics or reduced synaptic strength
  - Altered inhibitory/excitatory balance (changes in interneuron function)
  - Reduced plasticity (reduced LTP magnitude or probability)

- Adult post-TBI hippocampus (typical model features)
  - Acute cell loss in affected subregion (focal cell removal or decreased population size)
  - Reduced connectivity locally (synapse loss) and possible compensatory changes elsewhere
  - Increased spontaneous excitability / hyperexcitability (risk of network seizures)
  - Increased background noise (inflammatory milieu) that can be represented by increased synaptic noise or tonic current
  - Possible changes in conduction delays or axonal integrity (if available to model)

We can express each of the above as quantitative parameter changes (e.g., aging: -25% excitatory synapses in CA1; TBI: -40% cells in CA1 + +30% background noise). I can provide defaults if you want, or accept your experimental values.

## Suggested metrics to compute and compare
- Population firing rates and per-cell firing distributions
- Spike raster + spike-time synchrony / pairwise correlations
- Power spectra (theta/gamma bands), evoked LFP-like signals
- Synaptic currents and excitatory/inhibitory balance
- Input→output gain curves (stimulus-response)
- Measures of plasticity (if running plastic synapses): LTP magnitude, failure rates
- Network stability / seizure-like events (if TBI model produces hyperexcitability)

---

## Proposed default simulation parameters (can be modified)
- Simulation duration: 10–60 s (for spontaneous / oscillation analyses); shorter trials for paired or plasticity assays
- Time step (dt): 0.025–0.1 ms depending on neuron model complexity (smaller dt for Hodgkin–Huxley-like models)
- Number of neurons:

  - region-scale: tens of thousands (computationally heavy)

2. Once microcircuit results are consistent with expectations, scale up or design a region-scale simulation for more anatomically realistic comparisons.

platform_overview_capabilities.user: Explain what is the OpenBrain Institute virtual lab and what can I do in it exactly? Can I model a spiking neural network and run AI models (like reinforcement learning) on them in this platform?
platform_overview_capabilities.ai_response:
I'll search the platform documentation for \virtual labs\ to get authoritative details.I'll search documentation for \spiking", einforcement", and \simulations\ to check supported use-cases.## Summary — What the Open Brain Platform “Virtual Lab” (OBI‑one) is
- The Virtual Lab is a set of OBI‑one workflows and tooling for creating and managing reproducible, provenance‑tracked virtual laboratory environments for neuroscience modeling and simulation.
- It provides programmatic and web interfaces (notebooks / FastAPI / a web “Workflows” UI) to configure, generate and run model simulations and to organize research projects and parameter scans.

## Key capabilities (from the platform documentation)
- **Workflows & provenance**
  - Standardized workflows for building simulations (single cells, paired neurons, small microcircuits) and managing parameter scans and provenance.
  - Integration with the platform database via entitysdk.
- **Simulation types supported**
  - *Single neuron* simulations (biophysical ME-models / E-models).
  - *Paired neuron* simulations (two connected neurons).
  - *Small microcircuit* simulations (networks up to 20 biophysical neurons) and larger circuit support (microcircuit, region, etc.) via SONATA circuit format.
- **Circuit simulation features**
  - JSON‑schema driven configuration (duration, timestep, v_init, calcium, random seed, node_sets, stimuli, recordings, synaptic manipulations).
  - Node sets (AllNeurons, IDNeuronSet, PropertyNeuronSet, combinations) let you choose which neurons to simulate.
  - Built‑in synaptic mechanisms (e.g., ProbAMPANMDA_EMS, ProbGABAAB_EMS) and ability to apply *connection_overrides* and other synaptic manipulations.
  - Stimuli: current clamps, spike‑based inputs (Poisson, synchronous, sinusoidal), targeted stimuli to neuron sets.
  - Recordings: soma voltage traces, spike detection, time‑windowed recordings; outputs include spike files, HDF5, and NWB exports.
- **Execution backend**
  - Uses the BlueCelluLab/NEURON MPI execution backend with distributed (MPI) support and automated cell distribution.
- **User interfaces & programmatic use**
  - Web UI for configuration and monitoring (three‑column layout for small microcircuit simulations).
  - OBI‑one programmatic API / notebooks for automating configuration, campaign generation, and parameter scans.
- **Examples & templates**
  - Notebooks and example directories are available in OBI‑one (examples for small microcircuit simulations, paired neuron workflows, ion channel workflows, etc.).

## Important limitations noted in the documentation
- Plasticity: “There is currently no plasticity logic implemented in the platform data/model stack, so plasticity‑specific datasets or plasticity analysis outputs are not available at this time.”
- Some features/modules (e.g., a “Functional Networks module”) do not exist in the current codebase.
- The documentation describes biophysically‑detailed simulation workflows and exportable outputs, but it does **not** document an integrated facility for training machine‑learning agents (e.g., reinforcement‑learning agents) directly inside the virtual lab.

## Direct answer to your question: Can I model a spiking neural network and run AI models (like reinforcement learning) on them?
- Yes — you can model and simulate spiking neural networks (SNNs) at the scales documented:
  - Single neurons, paired neurons, and *small microcircuits* (up to 20 biophysical neurons) are explicitly supported with synapses, stimuli, and network recordings. Larger circuit types are represented in the system using SONATA circuits as well.
  - The simulation stack produces spike times, voltage traces, and NWB/HDF5 outputs suitable for analysis.
- Running reinforcement learning (RL) *agents inside the platform as a native, integrated workflow* is not documented:
  - The platform documentation does not describe an integrated RL training/evaluation framework or an API to attach an RL agent that acts on the simulated network and learns (i.e., online learning/closed‑loop agent training interacting with the simulator).
  - Plasticity mechanisms (needed for biologically plausible online learning rules) are currently not supported in the platform model stack, per the FAQ.
- Practical options / recommended approaches given the platform capabilities:
  - Option A — Use platform simulations as the environment and perform RL training externally:
    - Run network simulations to produce activity (spike trains, sensory inputs or responses).
    - Export simulation outputs (spike times, voltages, state files, or programmatic endpoints) and use them as data or environment traces for offline or offline‑online RL training using your external ML/agent tooling.
  - Option B — Implement agent‑in‑the‑loop externally via programmatic control (advanced):
    - Generate and run simulations programmatically with short simulation segments and external code that:
      - Reads observations (spikes, population rates) from the simulation outputs.
      - Computes actions with an RL agent externally.
      - Injects stimuli/inputs back into the simulation (e.g., by programmatically configuring the next simulation run with targeted inputs).
    - This is feasible if you orchestrate repeated simulation runs and data exchange, but the documentation does not describe a turnkey, low‑latency “agent in the loop” API for real‑time RL training.
  - Option C — If you need biologically plausible online synaptic plasticity (STDP, reward‑modulated plasticity): at present, the platform explicitly lacks plasticity logic — so native, biologically driven learning dynamics are not available per docs.

## Practical next steps I can help with
- If you want, I can:
  - Walk you through the recommended workflow to set up a small microcircuit simulation (web UI or OBI‑one notebook) and show how to export spike/voltage outputs.
  - Outline a concrete integration pattern to perform RL training externally while using the platform as the simulator/environment (examples of orchestration: run → export → agent update → run).
  - Search for any additional documentation pages or examples that specifically discuss programmatic orchestration or export formats you’d use for ML integration.
- Which would you like me to do next? (e.g., “Show me how to configure a small microcircuit in the web UI”, or “Give an example notebook pattern for running simulations and exporting spikes for RL training”.)

Sources: platform documentation pages retrieved (OBI‑one virtual labs overview, circuit simulation overview, small microcircuit simulation web interface, FAQ).

brain_structures_movement.user: What are the structures of the brain mainly involved in movement?
brain_structures_movement.ai_response:
## Overview
Movement is produced by a distributed network spanning cortex, subcortex, brainstem and spinal cord. Below are the main structures involved and their principal roles.

### Cortical areas
- **Primary motor cortex (M1; Brodmann area 4)**  
  - Directly issues motor commands; controls force and direction of voluntary movements via corticospinal and corticobulbar outputs.
- **Premotor cortex (lateral premotor areas)**  
  - Plans movements in response to external cues, selects actions, and coordinates reaching/arm movements.
- **Supplementary motor area (SMA; medial premotor cortex)**  
  - Initiation and sequencing of internally generated movements, bimanual coordination.
- **Primary somatosensory cortex (S1)**  
  - Provides sensory feedback (proprioception, touch) necessary for movement correction and fine control.

### Subcortical nuclei
- **Basal ganglia** (a functional group including:)
  - **Striatum (caudate + putamen)** — input stage; integrates cortical signals and context for action selection.  
  - **Globus pallidus (internal/external segments)** — output and relay nuclei that regulate thalamic drive to cortex.  
  - **Substantia nigra pars compacta (SNc)** — dopaminergic modulation crucial for movement initiation and vigor.  
  - **Subthalamic nucleus (STN)** — modulatory role in movement inhibition and switching.  
  - Function: action selection, initiation, suppression of unwanted movements, habit learning.
- **Thalamus (motor nuclei; e.g., ventrolateral nucleus)**  
  - Relays processed basal ganglia and cerebellar output back to motor cortical areas.

- **Cerebellum**  
  - Coordinates timing, precision and adaptation of movements; error correction through sensory feedback; important for balance and motor learning. Cerebellar output reaches cortex via thalamus.

### Brainstem centers
- **Brainstem motor nuclei and pathways** (reticular formation, vestibular nuclei, red nucleus)  
  - Mediate postural control, reflexes, gaze and head movements; integrate vestibular and proprioceptive signals; contribute descending pathways (reticulospinal, vestibulospinal) for posture and locomotion.
- **Red nucleus**  
  - Contributes to limb control (rubrospinal tract in some species) and receives cerebellar input.

### Spinal cord and peripheral motor system
- **Descending tracts (e.g., corticospinal tract)**  
  - Convey cortical motor commands to spinal interneurons and motor neurons; fine control of distal muscles, especially in primates.
- **Spinal cord circuits (central pattern generators, interneurons)**  
  - Generate rhythmic locomotor patterns (walking), reflexes and local coordination.
- **Lower motor neurons (ventral horn motor neurons) and neuromuscular junctions**  
  - Final common pathway that directly innervates muscles to produce contraction.

## Summary table
| Structure | Main role in movement |
|----------:|----------------------|
| **Primary motor cortex (M1)** | Execution of voluntary movement; corticospinal output |
| **Premotor / SMA** | Movement planning, sequencing, cue-driven vs internal initiation |
| **Basal ganglia** | Action selection, initiation/inhibition, motor learning |
| **Cerebellum** | Coordination, timing, error correction, motor adaptation |
| **Thalamus (motor nuclei)** | Relay of basal ganglia/cerebellar output to cortex |
| **Brainstem (reticular/vestibular nuclei, red nucleus)** | Posture, reflexes, locomotor control, gaze/head movement |
| **Spinal cord & CPGs** | Local reflexes, rhythmic locomotion, integration of descending commands |
| **Lower motor neurons** | Direct drive of muscles (final common pathway) |

If you’d like, I can:
- Provide a schematic diagram or image links showing these pathways,
- Describe the specific circuits for a movement (e.g., reaching, walking, eye movements),
- Or summarize clinical disorders that arise when each structure is damaged (e.g., Parkinson’s, cerebellar ataxia). Which would you prefer?

entitysdk_library.ai_response:
I'll look up the Context7 library ID for the entitysdk package and then fetch its documentation.I'll fetch the documentation for /openbraininstitute/entitysdk.## Summary — What is **entitysdk** in the Python notebook?

**entitysdk** (library ID: **/openbraininstitute/entitysdk**) is the Python client library used in notebooks to interact with the platform’s entitycore service. It provides a type-safe, intuitive API for authenticating, querying, searching, and managing scientific entities (e.g., cell morphologies, electrophysiology recordings, species, projects) and their assets from Python notebooks.

---

## Key features (from the documentation)
- Initialize a typed client that knows your project context and credentials.
- Search and iterate over entity types (e.g., **CellMorphology**, **Species**, **Strain**).
- Use environment or custom API endpoint configurations (staging, custom API URL).
- Examples and notebooks provided in the repository demonstrate searching, morphology access, and mesh/EM workflows.

---

## Typical usage in a notebook

1. Prepare authentication (token) and project context:
```python
from uuid import UUID
from entitysdk import Client, ProjectContext
from obi_auth import get_token  # optional for staging

# Example project context
project_context = ProjectContext(
    project_id=UUID(\12345678-1234-1234-1234-123456789012"),
    virtual_lab_id=UUID(\87654321-4321-4321-4321-210987654321")
)

# Get token (example using obi_auth for staging)
token = get_token(environment=\staging")
```

2. Initialize the client (environment or custom api_url):
```python
# Initialize with environment + token manager
client = Client(
    project_context=project_context,
    environment=\staging",
    token_manager=token
)

# OR initialize with a custom API URL and token string
client = Client(
    api_url=\https://api.custom.endpoint/v1",
    project_context=project_context,
    token_manager=\your-access-token-string"
)
```

3. Search for entities (example: morphologies):
```python
from entitysdk.models import CellMorphology

iterator = client.search_entity(
    entity_type=CellMorphology,
    query={\mtype__pref_label": \L5_TPC:A"},
    limit=1,
)
morphology = next(iterator)
```

---

## Example notebook references (documentation paths)
The docs and example notebooks referenced in the library include:
- examples/01_searching.ipynb — client initialization and searching examples
- examples/02_morphology.ipynb — morphology-specific operations
- examples/05_emcell_mesh.ipynb — EM cell mesh workflows
- README and other examples in the repository show initialization patterns (staging vs local API URL)

---

## Practical notes / tips
- Provide ProjectContext (project_id and virtual_lab_id) so queries are scoped to the correct project.
- Token management: you can pass a token string or a token manager helper (e.g., obi_auth.get_token for staging).
- The search API returns an iterator — use next() or iterate to fetch results; pass typed model classes (from entitysdk.models) to narrow queries.
- The library includes many code snippets and >100 examples in the repo (useful for notebook copy-paste).

---

If you want, I can:
- Show a minimal runnable notebook cell that authenticates (using environment-token) and lists the first 5 cell morphologies in your project.
- Or fetch a specific example notebook (e.g., examples/01_searching.ipynb) and show its key cells. Which would you like?

Notebook template (paste into a Jupyter cell):

```python
# MEModel single-cell simulation notebook (obi-one)
# - Replace memodel_ids[0] below with the MEModel ID you choose from the list I gave.
# - This builds and validates a simulation form. The Virtual Lab will execute it when submitted.

import obi_one as obi
from pathlib import Path

# --- Parameters ---
sim_duration = 3000.0  # ms
memodel_ids = [\e802fec0-9e6f-45e0-b36b-b9d8f372825f"]  # <-- replace with the MEModel ID you want

# --- Build empty MEModelSimulationScanConfig ---
sim_conf = obi.MEModelSimulationScanConfig.empty_config()

# Info block
info = obi.Info(campaign_name=\MEModel single-cell: seizure test", 
                campaign_description=\Single MEModel simulation for beginner seizure-relevant testing")
sim_conf.set(info, name=\info")

# Timestamps (regular repeated windows)
regular_timestamps = obi.RegularTimestamps(start_time=0.0, number_of_repetitions=1, interval=sim_duration)
sim_conf.add(regular_timestamps, name='RegularTimestamps')

# Stimulus: somatic constant current clamp (example)
stimulus = obi.ConstantCurrentClampSomaticStimulus(
    timestamps=regular_timestamps.ref,
    duration=2000.0,        # ms
    amplitude=0.2,          # nA (adjust to elicit spiking)
    delay=10.0              # ms before stimulus begins
)
sim_conf.add(stimulus, name=\CurrentClampInput")

# Recordings: soma voltage
voltage_recording = obi.SomaVoltageRecording()
sim_conf.add(voltage_recording, name='VoltageRecording')

# Optional: shorter time-window recording (example)
time_window_voltage_recording = obi.TimeWindowSomaVoltageRecording(start_time=0.0, end_time=2000.0)
sim_conf.add(time_window_voltage_recording, name='TimeWindowVoltageRecording')

# Initialization: point to your MEModel
init = obi.MEModelSimulationScanConfig.Initialize(
    circuit=obi.MEModelFromID(id_str=memodel_ids[0]),
    simulation_length=sim_duration,
    random_seed=1,
    v_init=-70.0  # mV starting potential
)
sim_conf.set(init, name='initialize')

# Validate the config
validated_sim_conf = sim_conf.validated_config()

# Print small summary
print(\Validated simulation configuration built.")
print(validated_sim_conf)
```

How to run in Virtual Lab (high-level):

At present, the public rat and mouse hippocampus ME-Models have the brain region `Field CA1`, e-type `cACpyr` (for rat models and `cADpyr` for mouse models) and m-type `SP_PC`. calcium_plateau_potentials_simulation.ai_response:
I'll search the ME-model database for models mentioning calcium (to find models with calcium mechanisms and apical dendrites).I'll broaden the search to models with \pyramidal\ in the name or description.## Summary of search so far
I searched the platform ME-model database for models matching \calcium\ and \pyramidal\ but found no matches in the first-page results. If you want, I can re-run more targeted searches (e.g., for specific species, brain region, or model authors) — would you like me to do that?

Below I give practical, platform-agnostic guidance for choosing an ME-model and the simulation parameters that reliably produce dendritic calcium-dependent plateau potentials in an apical dendrite. Use these as a checklist to (1) locate a suitable ME-model on the platform and (2) configure the simulation.

---

## 1) Which ME-model to choose (what to look for)
Look for a Morphology+Electrical (ME) model with these features (the platform search filters to use):

- **Cell type / name:** **pyramidal** (e.g., L5 or L2/3 pyramidal neuron) or explicitly labeled “apical” or “tuft” in morphology/emodel description.
- **Ion channels present:** high-threshold voltage-gated calcium channels (e.g., **CaL / CaHVA**) in apical dendrite, and **calcium dynamics** (intracellular [Ca++] state variable) plus **calcium-activated K+** currents (**SK**, **BK**, or KCa). These are essential for regenerative Ca plateau and subsequent repolarization.
- **Compartmental detail:** clearly resolved apical dendrite / tuft compartments (i.e., not a single truncated apical section).
- **Emodel/Etype metadata:** use the ME-model search filters for emodel__mtype__pref_label / emodel__etype__pref_label or check the model description for “calcium”, “Ca2+” or “SK”.
- **Validation / exemplar:** prefer models flagged as exemplar or with available validation traces.

If you want, I can re-run the platform ME-model search with specific species (e.g., *Mus musculus*), brain region (e.g., neocortex L5), or other constraints — tell me which and I’ll search again.

---

## 2) Key mechanisms & tolerances to verify in the model (check model internals)
- Presence of a calcium concentration state (e.g., internal [Ca2+]) with a decay time constant (tau_Ca) and shell volumes defined.
- High-threshold Ca channels (L-type, R-type, or HVA) expressed in apical compartments.
- Calcium-activated K channels (SK or BK) to reproduce after-plateau repolarization.
- NMDA receptor model or voltage-dependent synapses if you plan to drive plateau with synaptic input.
- Adequate axial resistance and compartment sizes so local dendritic depolarization is not instantly shunted to the soma.

---

## 3) Recommended stimulation strategies to evoke apical dendritic calcium plateau potentials
You can produce plateaus by either direct current injection into the apical dendrite or by clustered synaptic input (AMPA+NMDA) onto the tuft. Typical approaches:

A. Direct dendritic current injection
- **Injection site:** distal apical trunk or tuft compartment (where Ca channels are dense).
- **Current amplitude:** start with ~200–600 pA (model-dependent) for brief pulses; if the model is highly excitable, lower values may suffice.
- **Pulse duration:** 50–300 ms pulses to allow Ca channels to open and [Ca2+] to rise; longer pulses (200–500 ms) often produce sustained plateau.
- **Timing protocol:** single pulse, or short train of pulses (e.g., 3 pulses of 50–100 ms separated by 50–100 ms) to promote accumulation of depolarization and Ca.

B. Clustered synaptic input (physiological approach)
- **Synapse types:** AMPA + NMDA at the same dendritic location; NMDA’s voltage-dependence strongly supports plateau generation.
- **Number of synapses:** typically 30–200 nearby synapses, depending on strength; start ~50 synchronous inputs.
- **Synaptic timing:** synchronous or high-frequency volley (e.g., 50–200 Hz burst lasting 50–200 ms).
- **Background:** a modest level of somatic depolarization (holding soma at -65 to -60 mV) makes plateau induction easier.

Note: For reproducible plateaus, cluster inputs onto a short stretch of tuft (few tens of microns) rather than widely dispersed synapses.

---

## 4) Suggested simulation parameters (numerical & recording)
- **Simulator time step (dt):** 0.025 ms (0.01–0.05 ms acceptable). Use smaller dt if channel kinetics are fast or results look noisy.
- **Total simulation duration:** 500–2000 ms (include a baseline pre-stim period of 100–200 ms and post-stim 200–1000 ms to observe plateau and recovery).
- **Temperature:** 32–35 °C (if the model includes temperature dependence); otherwise use model default.
- **Integration method:** variable time step if supported (CVODE) or fixed dt with dt ≤ 0.025 ms.
- **Recording:** membrane potential at
  - **soma**
  - **apical trunk (midpoint)**
  - **apical tuft / distal site near stimulation**
  - intracellular **[Ca2+]** at the stimulation site (and maybe neighboring compartments)
- **Initial conditions:** hold at resting potential (e.g., -65 mV) for ≥100 ms before stimulus.

## 5) Parameters to tweak if plateau is absent or unstable
- **Increase local density of HVA Ca channels** in stimulated compartments (or choose a different compartment closer to the tuft).
- **Increase NMDA conductance** or the number of co-active synapses for synaptic induction.
- **Adjust intracellular Ca decay time (tau_Ca):** longer tau_Ca promotes accumulation and prolongation of plateau; shorter tau_Ca reduces plateau duration.
- **Adjust SK/KCa conductance:** increasing SK promotes termination and afterhyperpolarization; decreasing SK may make plateaus easier to sustain.
- **Modify axial resistance (Ra)** or local membrane leak (g_pas) if local depolarization is being shunted to the soma too fast.

---

## 6) Typical parameter set to try (example starting point)
| Parameter | Suggested value / range |
|---|---:|
| Simulation dt | 0.025 ms |
| Total duration | 1000 ms |

| Stimulus site | apical tuft (~distal apical branch) |

| Synaptic burst frequency | 50–200 Hz (for 50–200 ms) |

These values are starting points; the exact settings needed depend on the chosen ME-model.

- Save traces of Vm and Ca at the stimulated compartment and one proximal compartment to assess local vs. propagated effects.
- Plot dendritic [Ca2+] vs time alongside Vm to confirm the plateau is calcium-dependent.
- Run parameter sweeps across injected current amplitude and Ca decay time or SK conductance to map plateau existence/stability (the Open Brain Platform’s simulation campaign tools can help create such sweeps — tell me if you want a campaign configured).

m_vs_e_model_explanation.user: Explain the difference between m-model and e-model.
m_vs_e_model_explanation.expected_output:
The m-model is a detailed 3D reconstructed or synthesised morphology. A 3D reconstructed morphology is generated by tracing a microscopic images of biological neurons. The images are usally obtained using usally thin slices of tissue plated on a glass. Some reconstructed morphologies are obtained by `skeletonisation` of electron microscopic meshses (e.g. MiCRONS morphology meshes). The reconstructed morphologies usually undergo a pipeline to correct and fix for problems which can cause issues during simualtions. Synthesised morphologies are generated by topological synthesis using features from multiple morphologies. 
An e-model or electrical model is a usually a NEURON simulator-based single neuron model which is constructed by adding ion channel models to an reconstructed or synthesised morphology to generate electrical activity. The e-models are optimised using multi-objective optimisation (e.g. using BluePyEModel or BluePyOpt) to generate specific firing patterns as seen in real electrophysiological recordings. These models are also validated by using protocols and electrical features or e-features (e.g. using eFEL python library) which were not used for optimisation. E-models form a template for build more such neuron models by combining with morphologies (m-model) to generate me-models.  The main differences between an e-model and me-model is that e-models are optimised and non-simulatable (on the platform). Whereas me-models are formed by combining m-model and e-model, and can be simulated on the platform. The me-models are not optimised for any cobination of m-model and e-model.

Do you mean **m-model** as in a *morphology model* (morphology-only), or did you mean **ME-model** (morpho‑electric model, i.e., morphology + electrical model)? I couldn't find an exact glossary entry for the label \m-model\ — please confirm which term you want compared.

---

Below are the definitions I retrieved from the platform glossary to help you decide.

## Definitions (from platform glossary)
- **E-model**  
  *Definition:* An E-model, or electrical model, is a computational model that represents the electrical properties of a neuron. It is generated by fitting model parameters to experimental electrophysiological data such as membrane potential traces, generally through a multi-objective evolutionary algorithm that optimizes the model parameters. E-models are used to simulate and analyze neuronal excitability and response to stimuli. (Source: platform glossary)

- **ME-model**  
  *Definition:* An ME-model, or morphoelectric model, is a combination of an E-model with relevant morphology. ME-models can be simulated using BlueCelluLab and can serve as essential elements in circuit building. (Source: platform glossary)

## If you meant “E-model vs morphology-only (m-model)”
If by “m-model” you mean a morphology-only model (the neuronal reconstruction without fitted electrical parameters), the key differences are:

- **What they represent**
  - **Morphology-only (m-model)**: structure of the neuron — soma, dendrites, axon geometry and branching. No fitted electrophysiological dynamics.
  - **E-model**: membrane and channel dynamics, currents, and parameters fit to electrophysiological recordings. No explicit morphology required (often single-compartment or abstract electrical representation).

- **Primary use**
  - **Morphology**: anatomical analyses, compartment placement of channels/synapses, geometric measures.
  - **E-model**: reproducing spike waveforms, firing patterns, and responses to current injections.

- **When combined**
  - Combining morphology + E-model yields an **ME-model** (morphoelectric) that supports location-dependent electrical simulations (e.g., dendritic integration, synaptic input effects).

---

how_does_it_work.expected_output: The platform is organized around Virtual Labs containing Projects, where users can explore neuroscience data via a 3D brain atlas (Explore section), build computational neuron models (Workflows > Build), run simulations at various scales from single neurons to microcircuits (Workflows > Simulate), and analyze results in Jupyter notebooks. Below is a concise, structured overview of how the platform works based on the product documentation I retrieved.

### Core design principles
- **Workflows (OBI-one):** The platform provides standardized workflows (OBI-one) for building, configuring and running biophysically-detailed models (e.g., ion channel models, neuron skeletonization, paired-neuron and circuit simulations).
- **Database integration:** Workflows are integrated with a standardized cloud neuroscience database via the entity SDK (entitysdk), so models, data and provenance are stored and linked.
- **Provenance tracking:** The platform emphasizes standardized provenance for workflows and data—recording what was run/changed and how results were produced.
- **Parameter scans:** Workflows support standardized parameter scans so you can run systematic sweeps across model or simulation parameters.
- **API service:** Each workflow has a corresponding API (OpenAPI) service, enabling programmatic access and automation.

### Supported model & data types
- **Ion channel models:** Build, parameterize and validate ion channel descriptions for neuron simulations.
- **Neuron morphologies / skeletonization:** Process neuron morphologies, extract skeletons and analyze morphological properties for simulation readiness.
- **Paired-neuron simulations:** Workflows to configure and simulate pairs of interconnected neurons and analyze synaptic transmission.
- **Circuit (microcircuit) simulations:** Simulate networks of multiple biophysical neurons with synaptic connections and emergent dynamics.

### Circuit simulation specifics (from documentation)
- Circuit simulations model networks of neurons with synaptic edges and allow:
  - Multiple neurons (network composition)
  - Synaptic connections with synaptic mechanisms
  - Flexible neuron sets (selecting which neurons to simulate)
  - Synaptic manipulations (modify synaptic properties during simulations)
- Circuit simulations are configured with a JSON schema-based configuration system and support creation of simulation campaigns that run multiple related simulations.

### Interfaces & usage paths
- **Web interface:** The platform provides a web UI for small microcircuit simulations and other interactive workflows.
- **Programmatic interface (OBI-one):** Use the OBI-one library to script workflows and run simulations programmatically.
- **Notebooks & examples:** Example notebooks are provided in the OBI-one repository to demonstrate workflows and usage patterns.
- **FastAPI service:** Local FastAPI endpoints are available for many workflow components (docs accessible at a local /docs endpoint when run).

### Typical workflow (conceptual)
1. Prepare data / models: e.g., morphology preprocessing, channel configuration.
2. Configure simulation: use JSON schema (for circuits) or OBI-one APIs to define parameters and parameter scans.
3. Run workflows: execute via web UI or programmatically; the platform can run campaigns (multiple simulations).
4. Record provenance & results: database stores entities, configs and results with provenance metadata.
5. Analyze results: via provided notebooks, programmatic tools or saved outputs.

### Documentation & examples
- Each product area includes an Overview page, feature descriptions, installation notes, and example notebooks.
- FastAPI docs and example notebooks are recommended starting points for running and adapting workflows.

In other words, there are no built‑in models, stored protocols, or analysis outputs that implement synaptic plasticity (STDP, LTP/LTD, structural plasticity, or similar) in the platform data/model stack.

(Reference: platform FAQ retrieved from the documentation: “There is currently no plasticity logic implemented in the platform data/model stack, so plasticity‑specific datasets or plasticity analysis outputs are not available at this time.”)

---

## What you can do instead — adjacent data and resources available on the platform

Although dedicated plasticity features are not available, the platform contains many plasticity‑relevant datasets and digital models you can use as inputs for your own plasticity analyses or to implement plasticity in custom simulations. Relevant data types and resources available on the platform include:

- **Neuron morphology**  
  - Detailed soma/dendrite/axon reconstructions (useful for modeling location‑dependent plasticity and structural changes).
- **Electrophysiology recordings**  
  - Intracellular/electrophysiological traces and metadata (can be used to estimate spike timing, firing rate changes, and drive plasticity rules in simulations).
- **Ion channel models**  
  - Channel types and parameterizations (helpful to model how excitability changes interact with plasticity).
- **Synapse and connectivity data (synaptome)**  
  - Connection maps, synapse counts and distributions (useful baseline for applying plasticity rules that change synapse weights or numbers).
- **Synapses per connection experimental measurements**  
  - Empirical synapse counts per connection (to constrain models of synaptic scaling or structural plasticity).
- **Neuron and bouton density data**  
  - Spatial distributions and densities (inform homeostatic or structural plasticity constraints).
- **Digital single‑neuron and circuit models**  
  - E‑models (electrical), ME‑models (morphology + electrical), paired neuron and microcircuit configurations — these provide runnable models you could augment with plasticity rules if you implement them.
- **Simulation notebooks and workflows**  
  - Notebooks, example simulations, and state manipulation tools for assembling and running small microcircuit or paired‑neuron simulations (you can modify these to include plasticity mechanisms if you implement them client‑side).
- **Searchable literature and documentation**

Tip: use the platform browser/search to confirm the e-model / me-model and the ion-channel model IDs before configuring simulations.

---

## 2) Prepare two model variants
Create two concrete simulation inputs/ME-models:

- **Control (with channel)**  
  - Use the chosen ME-model as-is (all ion-channel mechanisms intact).
  - Keep the default parameterization of channel densities (or use the published/validated conductances).
  - **Label** this variant clearly (e.g., \Pyr_CA1_control_with_Ih").

- **Channel-absent (without channel)** — two practical ways:
  1. **Remove mechanism** from the cell model (preferred if platform supports editing the ME-model composition): remove the ion-channel mechanism entry from the cell/e-model so it is not present at all.
  2. **Zero conductance** (safer if you cannot edit mechanisms): set the channel’s maximal conductance (g_max) to 0 in every compartment where it appears. This keeps the mechanism present but effectively disables it.

Notes:
- Removing the mechanism may change gating-variable state initialization; zeroing g_max tends to be safer for comparability.
- If the channel has spatially varying density, ensure you apply the removal/zeroing consistently across compartments (soma, dendrite, axon) as appropriate.

---

## 3) Simulation protocol (identical for both variants)
Use the same protocol for control and channel-absent runs.

Recommended basic protocols:
- Resting membrane check:
  - No stimulus, record membrane potential for ~500–1000 ms to check v_rest and stability.
- Current step protocol:
  - Inject a family of somatic current steps (e.g., 50–500 ms steps) to generate an f–I curve:
    - Steps: 0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300 pA (or tuned to model excitability)
    - Duration: 500 ms
    - Interval between steps: 1 s (or run separate simulations)
- Subthreshold / ramp protocol:
  - Slow depolarizing ramp (useful for Ih and persistent currents).
- Synaptic-like input:
  - Poisson synaptic bombardment or a few conductance-based synapses (assess integration).
- Spike timing / paired-pulse tests:
  - Short trains to probe spike-frequency adaptation or recovery.

Essential recording settings:
- Somatic membrane potential (Vm) — high temporal resolution (dt <= 0.1 ms for spikes).
- Optionally dendritic Vm at representative compartments (apical tuft, proximal apical, basal).
- Spike times (detect threshold crossings).
- If available, gating variable traces or ionic currents for the tested channel (to confirm elimination).

Example minimal simulation parameters:
| Parameter | Typical value |
|---|---|
| Simulation duration | 1000 ms (adjust per protocol) |
| Integration dt | 0.025–0.1 ms |
| Temperature | match the model (e.g., 34°C or 37°C) |
| Initial Vm (v_init) | model default or e.g., -70 mV |
| Random seed | set for reproducibility |

---

## 4) Running the simulations on the platform
(High-level steps — exact UI/API calls depend on what you want me to prepare.)

- Create two single-neuron simulation configurations (or a simulation campaign with two simulations):
  - Circuit / SimulationCampaign is not required for single neuron; use single-neuron simulation endpoint or the \single neuron simulation\ builder.
- Assign the appropriate ME-model or e-model to each run.
- Set the stimuli and recording blocks identically for both.
- Optionally enable saving of ionic currents / gating variables for the tested channel when present (so you can confirm its activity in control and absence in test).

If you want, I can:
- Draft the platform shared state to define these two simulations (I can patch the state), or
- Prepare the simulation configuration JSON for you to upload, or
- Walk you through the exact UI steps (note: I cannot see the UI, but I can prepare the files and platform-API calls).

Would you like me to prepare the simulation config now? If yes, tell me:
- Which pyramidal model (CA1 or CA3, or a specific model ID), or allow me to pick a generic CA1 pyramidal ME-model from the platform.
- The ion-channel name/ID you want removed (or say “I’ll pick Ih / HCN”).
- Which stimulation protocol (current steps, ramp, synaptic input) you prefer.

---

## 5) Analysis and comparisons
Key analyses to compare control vs channel-absent:

- Basic electrophysiology:
  - Resting potential, input resistance (from subthreshold steps), membrane time constant.
- Spike generation:
  - Rheobase (minimum current to evoke a spike).
  - Spike frequency vs current (f–I curve).
  - Spike waveform (AP amplitude, half-width).
  - Adaptation index (ISIs over long steps).
- Subthreshold integration:
  - PSP amplitude and decay (for dendritic channels).
  - Resonance / sag (e.g., disappearance of Ih sag when HCN removed).
- Ionic-level checks:
  - In control: confirm channel currents/gating dynamics are present.
  - In test: confirm zero current or gating not activated.
- Statistical or visualization suggestions:
  - Overlay voltage traces for matched stimuli.
  - Plot f–I curves with error bars if multiple seeds/realizations.
  - Compute difference traces (control − test) for quantifying changes.

---

## 6) Reproducibility and validation tips
- Use explicit random seeds for stochastic inputs and report them.
- Keep all other model parameters identical between variants.
- If you zero g_max, also check derived variables (e.g., reversal potential unaffected).
- Run a small sensitivity analysis (vary conductance from 0 → baseline → 2× baseline) to confirm monotonic effects and exclude simulation artifacts.
- Save model versions and metadata (which platform entity IDs were used) so results are reproducible.

---

## 7) Common pitfalls
- Forgetting to zero/remove the channel in all compartments where it exists → residual effect.
- Removing model mechanism but not reinitializing state variables → transient differences at t=0.
- Different initialization random seeds between variants (can confound comparisons).
- Using too large dt for spike waveform analysis (use dt ≤ 0.1 ms).

---

**Key capabilities (from the circuit‑simulation documentation and OBI-one):**

- **Small Microcircuit Simulation (Web Interface)**

  - **Synaptic mechanisms:** Built‑in synaptic mechanisms (examples: ProbAMPANMDA_EMS, ProbGABAAB_EMS) with probabilistic release and mini events; synaptic manipulations (overrides) are supported (e.g., scaling neuromodulatory effects).
